_comment: Per-language localization for generated stories. `terms` swap culturally-specific proper nouns (place names, currency) before translation so each language gets a native setting; the `en` exonym is shown as the English reference, the `target` native spelling is fed to MT. `culturalNote` fully overrides the English note with one describing the target culture (kept in English for the learner). Keyed by the source (en-…) story id; language keys are LibreTranslate codes (es/fr/de/it/sv). Anything omitted falls back to the English source.

es.body: In Spain, small towns (pueblos) are cherished for their slower rhythm, where neighbours greet one another in the plaza and daily life centres on family, school and the local shops.

fr.body: In France, small towns and villages are prized for their gentle pace of life, where daily routines revolve around family, the school and the village square or weekly market.

de.body: In Germany, small towns (Kleinstädte) are valued for their calm, orderly pace, where residents often know their neighbours and weekends centre on family and local clubs (Vereine).

it.body: In Italy, small towns (paesi) are loved for their slower pace, where neighbours meet in the piazza and daily life revolves around family, school and the local shops.

sv.body: In Sweden, small towns are valued for their calm and their closeness to nature, where life moves at an unhurried pace and weekends often centre on family and the outdoors.

es.body: In Spain, Sunday is traditionally for family. Many gather for a long midday meal (la comida), and visiting grandparents over the weekend is a cherished custom.

fr.body: In France, Sunday is traditionally a family day. Many families share a long déjeuner together, and visiting grandparents is a common weekend tradition.

de.body: In Germany, Sunday is a quiet family day when most shops stay closed. Families often gather for Kaffee und Kuchen in the afternoon and visit grandparents.

it.body: In Italy, Sunday is dedicated to family. Many gather for a long pranzo della domenica, and visiting the nonni over the weekend is a treasured custom.

sv.body: In Sweden, weekends often bring families together for fika — coffee and something sweet — and a relaxed visit to grandparents.

es.body: Open-air markets (mercados and mercadillos) remain popular across Spain. Many towns hold a weekly market where locals buy fresh produce, bread and flowers directly from the sellers.

fr.body: The weekly market (le marché) is a fixture of French life. In most towns, locals shop for fresh produce, bread, cheese and flowers directly from the stallholders.

de.body: Weekly markets (Wochenmärkte) are popular across Germany. On market day, locals buy fresh produce, bread and flowers directly from the sellers on the town square.

it.body: The open-air market (il mercato) is part of daily life in Italy. Many towns hold a weekly market where locals buy fresh produce, bread and flowers from the vendors.

sv.body: Outdoor markets (torghandel) are a familiar sight on town squares across Sweden, where locals buy fresh produce, bread and flowers from the stallholders, especially in summer.

es.body: Hiking (senderismo) is a much-loved weekend pastime in Spain, from the Pyrenees to the Picos de Europa. Walkers set out with a packed lunch and often end the day at a village bar.

fr.body: Hiking (la randonnée) is a popular weekend activity in France, from the Alps to the Pyrenees. Walkers set out with a picnic and often finish the day in a mountain café.

de.body: Hiking (Wandern) is a beloved weekend pastime in Germany, especially in regions like the Black Forest and the Alps. Walkers carry a packed lunch and often stop at a mountain hut (Hütte).

it.body: Hiking (escursionismo) is a popular weekend pastime in Italy, from the Dolomites to the Apennines. Walkers set out with a packed lunch and often end the day in a mountain rifugio.

sv.body: Spending the weekend in nature is central to Swedish life, supported by allemansrätten, the right to roam. Walkers head out with a packed lunch and a thermos, whatever the weather.

es.body: Spain has many world-class museums, and several offer free entry at certain times — including Madrid's Prado on weekday evenings and many of Barcelona's museums on the first Sunday of the month.

fr.body: France has many celebrated museums, and the national ones — including the Louvre and the Musée d'Orsay in Paris — offer free entry on the first Sunday of the month.

de.body: Germany is rich in museums, especially in Berlin, home to the Museumsinsel. Many offer reduced or free admission at certain times, and entry is often free for children and students.

it.body: Italy is home to extraordinary museums and sites, and state-run ones — including the Colosseum and the Roman Forum in Rome — offer free entry on the first Sunday of the month.

sv.body: Sweden made many of its national museums free to enter, including several in Stockholm such as the Nationalmuseum and the Moderna Museet, so visitors can explore world-class collections without paying admission.

es.body: In many Spanish workplaces, a new employee's first day includes a tour, introductions to the team and often a relaxed coffee together. Colleagues tend to be warm and approachable.

fr.body: In many French workplaces, a new colleague's first day includes introductions to the team and often a welcoming coffee. It is common to greet each colleague individually.

de.body: In many German workplaces, a new employee's first day is well organised, with a tour, introductions and a clear handover of tasks. Punctuality and asking questions are valued.

it.body: In many Italian workplaces, a new colleague's first day includes a tour, introductions to the team and often a coffee together at the bar. Colleagues tend to be welcoming.

sv.body: In many Swedish workplaces, a new employee's first day includes introductions and a shared fika with the team. Workplaces tend to be informal and consensus-driven, and questions are welcome.
